articles.title: Council Tax Going Up in England
articles.summary: Council tax bills in England will go up by 4.9% on average starting in April. This means people will pay more money for local services like libraries and bin collection. The average bill for a Band D home will be £2,392 a year, which is £111 more than last year. Councils are raising taxes because they need more money to pay for services, especially to help vulnerable people. Some places like Shropshire will see bigger increases, while others like Hartlepool will have smaller ones. The increase is higher than the inflation rate of 3%, making things more expensive for families. Council tax is a charge on properties that funds local services, and councils are allowed to raise it by up to 5% in many cases.

articles.title: Hospital Delay in Meningitis Outbreak Alert
articles.summary: A hospital in the UK waited two days before telling health officials about a meningitis outbreak. Meningitis is a serious illness that can spread quickly. The hospital, called Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother Hospital, had a patient with meningitis on Wednesday, but they did not report it until Friday. This delay meant that people who were close to the patient could not be warned right away. Experts say this was a mistake because hospitals must report suspected cases immediately, not wait for test results. Two people died, and four were in intensive care. The hospital admitted they should have acted sooner to protect others.

articles.title: A Woman Becomes the Top Leader of the Church of England
articles.summary: Dame Sarah Mullally is becoming the first female Archbishop of Canterbury, which is a big job leading the Church of England. She will have a special ceremony at Canterbury Cathedral. Many important people, like the Prince and Princess of Wales, will be there to watch. Dame Sarah used to be a nurse, and she invited nurses and carers to the event. This is a historic moment because all the previous archbishops were men. Women were only allowed to become priests in the Church of England starting in 1994. Dame Sarah says she wants to celebrate women and show others they can follow their dreams too. Before the ceremony, she walked a long way on a pilgrimage to the cathedral.

articles.title: Fixing Teeth: Can the NHS Make Dentists Easier to Find?
articles.summary: This article talks about problems with NHS dentistry, which is the public dental service in the UK. Many people, like Carol in Bristol, have to wait in long lines early in the morning just to sign up for a dentist because it's hard to get an appointment. The NHS started in 1948 to provide free healthcare, but dental care quickly had small charges added to control costs. Today, patients pay fees for treatments like checkups and fillings, but the bigger issue is that lots of people can't find a dentist who will take them as NHS patients. The government is planning changes to make it easier to access dental care, but experts like Mark Dayan worry that without big fixes, the system might not work well in many areas. The article asks if these new plans will help save NHS dentistry for millions who need it.

articles.title: Denmark's Election Leaves Prime Minister's Future Uncertain
articles.summary: In Denmark, an election happened on Tuesday, but the results were not clear. This means no group won enough votes to decide who will be the prime minister. The current prime minister, Mette Frederiksen, leads the Social Democrats party, but they got fewer votes than before. Another important person, Lars Løkke Rasmussen, leads the Moderate party and can help decide who becomes prime minister. He wants everyone to work together. The election focused on issues like money and living costs, not on a big problem about Greenland and the U.S. president. Denmark is a small country in Europe with about 6 million people, and it is part of groups like the European Union and NATO. The prime minister is known for helping Ukraine and having strict rules on migration. Now, people are waiting to see if she can stay as prime minister or if someone else will take over.

articles.title: U.S. Sends Soldiers to the Middle East
articles.summary: The United States is sending about 1,000 soldiers from the Army's 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East soon. These soldiers are part of a special team that can go quickly to help in emergencies. They are trained to jump from planes into dangerous areas to protect important places like airports. This move adds to other U.S. troops already heading to the region. The information comes from a secret source who told a news group. The soldiers are led by a general and include a combat team. This shows the U.S. is getting ready to respond to problems in that part of the world.
